skill.name: tdd
skill.description: Guide AI coding agents through test-driven development using a red-green-refactor loop, behavior-focused tests, testable interfaces, and careful mocking boundaries.
skill.summary: Use a disciplined TDD workflow that tests behavior through public interfaces before implementation.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, environment access, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and a critical combination heuristic. Review found these are markdown explanations, code-fence examples, diagrams, or keyword matches, with no executable skill scripts and no prompt injection attempts. No evidence found of malicious intent, credential access, data exfiltration, or unsafe runtime behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are markdown code fences and instructional examples. They are not executable scripts and do not run commands when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are fenced markdown examples or diagrams. No file contains executable shell, Ruby, or automation code at these locations.

low_findings.title: Example Network and Environment Access Dismissed
low_findings.description: The fetch calls and STRIPE_KEY reference appear inside TypeScript examples about dependency injection and mockable API boundaries. They illustrate testing design and do not perform real network or environment access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is contained inside markdown documentation and is explicitly labeled as easy or hard to mock. There is no runnable package, script, or import path that executes the example.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported blocker findings are keyword collisions in prose and example text. Review found no cryptographic implementation, hashing routine, system probing command, or network reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited locations found testing guidance, headings, and diagrams only. No security-sensitive algorithm or reconnaissance instruction is present.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined separate static signals for examples of commands, network calls, and credentials. In context, those signals are documentation-only examples and do not combine into executable behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only network and environment references are pedagogical snippets, and command indicators are markdown fences. No evidence found for obfuscation, exfiltration, or malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Teams often write tests after implementation or tie tests to internals. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through behavior-first TDD with small red-green-refactor cycles.
actual_capabilities: Plans TDD work around public interfaces and user-visible behavior.
Distinguishes behavior tests from implementation-detail tests.
Guides interface design for easier testing and mocking.
Limits mocks to system boundaries such as APIs, databases, time, and files.
Identifies refactor candidates after tests are passing.
limitations: Does not provide framework-specific test runner commands.
Requires the user to confirm important behaviors before implementation.
Does not replace project-specific test architecture decisions.
Does not guarantee complete test coverage for every edge case.

use_cases.title: Build a New Feature Test First
use_cases.description: Define the public interface, write one failing behavior test, implement the minimum code, and repeat until the feature is complete.

use_cases.title: Fix a Bug With Regression Coverage
use_cases.description: Capture the bug as a failing test through the public interface, then implement the smallest change that makes the behavior pass.

use_cases.title: Improve Test Design During Refactors
use_cases.description: Review tests for implementation coupling, unsafe mocks, and unclear boundaries before changing internal design.

prompt_templates.title: Start a TDD Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan this feature with TDD. First ask what public interface should change and which behaviors matter most.

prompt_templates.title: Write the First Failing Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the TDD skill to write one failing test for the most important behavior. Keep it focused on the public interface.

prompt_templates.title: Continue the Red-Green Loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Continue with the next TDD cycle. Add one behavior test, implement only enough code to pass, then report what changed.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Tests Before Refactoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these tests using the TDD skill. Identify implementation coupling, unnecessary mocks, weak behavior coverage, and safe refactor steps.

output_examples.input: I need to add checkout discounts with TDD.
output: A short behavior list ranked by user impact.
One first failing test described through the checkout interface.
A minimal implementation plan for the green step.
Follow-up cycles for edge cases and refactoring.

output_examples.input: My tests break whenever I rename private methods.
output: A diagnosis that tests are coupled to implementation details.
Suggested behavior-focused test names and assertions.
Mocking changes that keep internal collaborators real.
A refactor order that keeps the suite passing.

output_examples.input: Should I mock this payment gateway in my tests?
output: A boundary decision based on whether the dependency is external.
A testability recommendation using dependency injection.
A mock shape tied to one clear operation.
A warning against mocking modules owned by the project.

best_practices: Confirm the public interface and highest-value behaviors before writing tests.
Write one failing behavior test, then implement only enough code to pass it.
Refactor only after the relevant tests are green.
anti_patterns: Writing all tests before learning from the first implementation cycle.
Testing private methods, call counts, or internal collaborators as primary behavior.
Mocking project-owned modules instead of exercising real code paths.

faq.question: What testing style does this skill encourage?
faq.answer: It encourages integration-style tests that verify behavior through public interfaces.

faq.question: Does it always avoid mocks?
faq.answer: No. It recommends mocks at system boundaries such as external APIs, time, databases, and files.

faq.question: Can it help with existing code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can add regression tests, reduce implementation coupling, and guide safer refactors.

faq.question: Does it choose a test framework?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on workflow and test design, then adapts to the framework already in your project.

faq.question: How does it handle planning?
faq.answer: It asks the user to confirm interface changes and the behaviors that matter most.

faq.question: Is this useful for Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a clear test-first workflow for coding tasks.
